`force_lazy=True` means that the test is intended to fail if any non-lazy execution is triggered in the process.

this is an annotation though, and `__annotations__` for a class is a dict where the keys are the names of the attributes, and the values are the types of the attributes:

`tests/test_annotations.py`:

```python
class URIRef:
    pass


class URIRefB(URIRef):
    pass


class MyClass:
    a: URIRef
    b: URIRefB


def test_types() -> None:
    assert issubclass(URIRefB, URIRef)
    assert not isinstance(URIRefB, URIRef)


def test_annotations() -> None:
    assert MyClass.__annotations__.keys() == {"a", "b"}

    for key, term in MyClass.__annotations__.items():
        assert isinstance(key, str)
        assert issubclass(term, URIRef)
        assert not isinstance(term, URIRef)

    ann = MyClass.__annotations__
    assert ann["a"] == URIRef
    assert issubclass(ann["a"], URIRef)
    assert not issubclass(ann["a"], URIRefB)
    assert not isinstance(ann["a"], URIRef)

    assert ann["b"] == URIRefB
    assert issubclass(ann["b"], URIRef)
    assert issubclass(ann["b"], URIRefB)
    assert not isinstance(ann["b"], URIRef)
    assert not isinstance(ann["b"], URIRefB)
```

@camelpac that's a good point. If we want to make `qty` optional while maintaining the current order of arguments, we could consider one of these approaches:

1. Make `qty` optional, leave `qty` in its current position, then add `notional` as the last kwarg. This maintains order at the expense of presenting the required `side`, `type`, and `time_in_force` arguments as optional.
2. Rename `qty` to something like `size`, then add `size_type` as the last kwarg, which accepts `"qty"` or `"notional"` and defaults to `"qty"`. This sidesteps the issue of making `side`, `type`, and `time_in_force` optional, at the expense of possibly breaking code that passes an explicitly named `qty` argument to `submit_order`.

Things like scale factor and add offset in a geotiff are not standard so I don't think we should add any support for that. Support for that should be added in a separate reader (a custom reader created by the user).

Geotiffs, at least those created by GDAL/rasterio, have `nodata` values set that should be masked. That should be considered the same as `_FillValue` in my opinion. In the future I'd like this reader to use the `rioxarray` library if possible to read GDAL datasets as xarray DataArrays.

As for not knowing if something is a category, assume that if it is an integer that there is a `nodata` value that can be used as `_FillValue`. If it integer and has an alpha band or has no `nodata` value specified, then don't set `_FillValue` and leave it as is.

In other readers, things like `units` are just metadata to fill in the space of missing information in the file. In those cases, the reader is specific to a particular implementation of a data file (VIIRS SDR in the HDF5 format versus generic HDF5 files) or are representing a standard (CF compliant NetCDF files). This generic image reader should try to use standard geotiff features.

At this point, I wonder if we are actually talking about different things. I just want to make sure we are on the same page, could you please define the expected behavior when no dependency is passed to this function? 
In this PR, the behavior is defined as follows:
Suppose a user wants to run a command without any dependencies e.g. `date`, when he/she adds `--memoize` options in CLI (`cl run date --memoize`), if there is no previously created bundle, we return an empty list here. Otherwise, we want to return the most recently finished bundle that uses the same command `cl run date`.
In SQL, if we use the same JOIN query on the case when no dependency is specified as follows, we won't be able to retrieve any bundles even if there is one created before.
```
mysql> SELECT * FROM bundle JOIN bundle_dependency ON bundle.uuid = bundle_dependency.child_uuid WHERE bundle.command = 'date';
Empty set (0.00 sec)
```
Unless we do a **LEFT JOIN**:
```
mysql> SELECT uuid, child_uuid FROM bundle LEFT JOIN bundle_dependency ON bundle.uuid = bundle_dependency.child_uuid WHERE bundle.command = 'date';
+------------------------------------+------------+
| uuid                               | child_uuid |
+------------------------------------+------------+
| 0xe4cbf69320fa460ba151e18e4e0324b1 | NULL       |
| 0xb66da75ffb834050a3f8d5b8637fe3fb | NULL       |
| 0xfae685c553a9451eb22a4803b6e5da05 | NULL       |
 +------------------------------------+------------+
3 rows in set (0.00 sec)
```
But then this will be different than the **INNER JOIN** that we are currently using when user specified the dependencies. Let me know how you think.

This was an attempt to ensure we always provide a meaningful error message to the user and aggregate them so multiple error messages can be shown at once. So we can receive json responses that look like this:

`{"startday": [
        "This field is required."
    ]}`
So in this case, it'll look up the 'startday' key in prettyErrorMessages and use that message instead.

Or an error message that looks like this:
`{
    "tree": [
        "moz does not exist."
    ]
}`
In this case, we'll return this message since it'd be useful to show the user they entered in the wrong tree (repo) query param

Or in the case of the 503:
[503: "Service Unavailable"] from the `getData` method we'll return a less cryptic message instead.

Looking at it now, it could be simplified with the aggregation part of it moved into the `ErrorMessages` component.

Maybe we should have a discussion about how we want to handle error messaging for TH in general, since error message quality varies depending on whether we receive a meaningful json response or any json response at all?

I see that you're trying to reduce the number of calls to `swagger_spec.deref`, but I don't see the reason for that other than performances.

If performances is your leading reason I would suggest to check https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/pull/305#discussion_r234901332 as it is really similar to what you are doing here.

The base point is that to improve performances you are duplicating a lot of code and this makes the code-base harder to maintain as changes in `unmarshal_schema_object` will not be reflected on the code that you copy-pasted and this could lead to inconsistent/surprising behaviour.

I would suggest to cache the `swagger_spec.deref` method (actually it's internal implementation provided in `bravado_core.spec.Spec._force_deref`).
If you do so you'll not reduce the number of calls to `deref` but you could make them faster.

This seems like it will blow up if the caller supplies `timeout_time` on their own as a keyword arg when they call the function, and will be silently ignored if they supply it on their own as a positional argument. I would say in general it's unusual for a decorator to supply more than `*args, **kwargs` to the called func. If this decorator is really a helper to supply arguments like `timeout_time` and `last_exception` to a function, and these args are really intended to be *non-public* (i.e. nothing but the decorator should supply them), then I would suggest:

* removing them as named parameters of the wrapped functions
* only passing them via kwargs
* prefixing the names with underscores `"_timeout_time"` and `"_last_exception"`

That's a little unusual, but makes it clear that no caller is supposed to provide these values (and all makes it less likely that anyone/anything will try to). 

Alternatively, it's possible there is some other design that does not require the wrapped function to accept these values directly itself. I would actually say that is worth investigating.

According to docs https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/winevt/nf-winevt-evtformatmessage

> _BufferUsed_
The size, in **characters** of the caller-allocated buffer that the function used or the required buffer size if the function fails with ERROR_INSUFFICIENT_BUFFER.


so the `returned_size ` variable, which is given as a `BufferUsed` parameter, should be an amount of characters, shouldn't it?

Without those changes, our app had been constantly crashing, and that's(as I assume) why:
* `EvtFormatMessage` sets some value for the `returned_size`, for example `10` characters.

* Then we allocate memory for 10 **bytes**, not characters.
_This is a previous code._
```
	allocated_size *= sizeof(WCHAR);
	buf = (WCHAR *)malloc(allocated_size);
```
So we have a buffer with a size - 10 bytes, but the size of the string is 10 wide characters(20 bytes)
* Then:
_This is a previous code._
```
buf[returned_size * sizeof(WCHAR)] = L'\0';
```
we try to set zero termination character by index 20 in the buffer with size 10 bytes. The result - segfault.

It also seems like we should `returned_size - 1`. You can see that in the official [docs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/wes/formatting-event-messages) too.

Okay, I think that this is safe, but I don’t see how it reads the
correct file. On Windows, `__file__` is like `C:\Users\someone\...`, but
the `posixpath.dirname` of that value is the empty string, because it
contains no POSIX path separators. So `norm_path` ends up being just the
NT-to-POSIX-converted input path without any directory prefix at all,
which behaves correctly under the safety check but won’t actually be
read from the filesystem correctly (because it will be a relative path,
and the current working directory will not be `dirname(__file__)`).

Do you think that it would make more sense to use `os.path.normpath`,
given that we’re trying to sanitize access to the platform-specific file
system? Something like:

```python
resource_name = os.path.normpath(static_path_part)
if not resource_name.startswith("static" + os.path.sep):
  return ...
resource_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), resource_name)
with open(resource_path, "rb") as infile:
  contents = infile.read()
...
```

I had hoped that `posixpath.normpath` could make this behavior more
portable, but I guess given a URL with encoded backslashes there isn’t
really a clearly portable way to handle it (that I can see—?).

In the old code, only 8 bits of each 64 bit buckets were used, but eight times as many buckets as necessary were also used. That's why this mistake didn't affect the correctness of the bloom filter. So in the correct form, the same number of bytes are actually written to, but gaps are eliminated.

If something should have used 10 bytes, for example, it would end up using 80 bytes in memory, but only 1/8 of those bytes would be written to. If those bytes are extracted and re-assembled in contiguous order, they represent the same data as in the legacy format, just without the empty gaps. 

Kind of like this:

```
A0000000 B0000000 C0000000 D0000000
... becomes ...
ABCD
```

If I understand this correctly, we're taking an image name (which somehow we're promised will include the image's SHA256), hashing that, and using it as an ID. 

Why not just use the Docker image ID (which is its SHA256) directly? It would be easier to reason about during debugging, for one thing. If I see a volume called `pants-named-caches-c0668a6d2dae` I know it is associated with that image, and not with some image that has an expanded name that hashes to that (how would I then find that image?)

Furthermore, the same image may have multiple names, the ID is the canonical thing. We don't need separate named caches for each different way of referencing the same image.

I don't know how the general docker environment code identifies images, but it seems like it should be by ID, not by name, of which, again, their can be many.

- `library.tools` contains *useful* things that are not necessarily part of the core library
- At no place within `library` does it import from `library.tools` (i.e. `library.tools` could in theory be deleted from the codebase and the library code would remain functional)

Let's make it more concrete: the SBU dataset provides a `SBUCaptionedPhotoDataset.tar.gz` archive, which contains a list of URLs for images and corresponding captions. Thus, before you can use the dataset, we need download all of these images. In #5683 we opted to put this functionality into the `preprocess` stage. Thus, when the dataset is loaded the first time, the archive is downloaded and the preprocessing extracts it and starts the download of all the images. Since we are not repackaging the images, we put the original data as well as the downloaded images into a `SBUCaptionedPhotoDataset` folder. With this, our automatic resource loading automatically picks the extracted folder to load the resource and thus also load the images.

> In the example you describe, `foo.tar` was removed during the "preprocessing" stage? Or just not created at all in the mock because we don't actually need it?

TL;DR: `foo.tar` is not created by the test suite.

In the test suite, we cannot perform the preprocessing, because it would require internet access and would take quite some time even if we provide fake images (this is what @lezwon had as a first draft). Thus, the easiest way to provide mock data is to provide the folder `SBUCaptionedPhotoDataset` directly rather than `SBUCaptionedPhotoDataset.tar.gz`. Thus, if the dataset loads the resource it is tricked into loading the "already preprocessed folder" rather than the raw archive.

I had not chosen 17 by chance. 17 digits is the minimal number of digits for which the chain of conversions binary->ascii->binary is guaranteed to be exact. See the the [definition of `DBL_DECIMAL_DIG` (C11/C++17)](https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/types/limits).

Your calculus is right:
```
  log10(2^53)=15.95...
```
That means that you need at least 16 digits to correctly represent in ASCII a binary `double`, otherwise one looses precision. But, for a technical reason (denormals), one more digit is needed. See also the Wikipedia page  [IEEE_754, section Character representation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IEEE_754#Character_representation).

Ok looking into this more closely tonight, I think I figured out:

1. why `verbose` is not being respected
2. why it seems like ONLY `verbose` is not being respected and all other parameters are working correctly

## Short Summary

Two bugs in the R package:

1. `Dataset$slice()` calls `LGBM_DatasetGetSubset()`, which will globally set the log level to INFO if `verbosity` isn't in the parameters passed to it. And `verbosity` is never passed through.
2. When `Dataset$update_params(new_params)` is called on a `Dataset` that has already been constructed, it doesn't store `new_params` in memory in the R object (`private$params`)

I just pushed https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4903/commits/619eaf484232642d9e231a5186a1d2cf2c48d74a which reverts the addition of `booster$reset_parameter()` and adds fixes for these two bugs.

## Much Longer Summary

I was able to reduce the internals of `lgb.cv()` to the following code that helps narrow down the issue.

<details><summary>sample code (click me)</summary>

```r
library(lightgbm)

data("agaricus.train", package = "lightgbm")

params <- list(
    max_bin = 123
    , verbose = -7L
)

dtrain <- lgb.Dataset(
    data = agaricus.train$data
    , label = agaricus.train$label
    , params = params
)
dtest <- slice(
    dataset = dtrain
    , idxset = seq_len(100L)
)

# with a validation set, you get logs
booster_w_valid <- lightgbm:::Booster$new(
    params = params
    , train_set = dtrain
)
booster_w_valid$add_valid(data = dtest, name = "valid")
booster_w_valid$update()

# without a validation set, no logs!
booster_no_valid <- lightgbm:::Booster$new(
    params = params
    , train_set = dtrain
)
booster_no_valid$update()
```

</details>

root cause: The use of `slice()` on a `Dataset` resets LightGBM's log level globally to the default value (INFO).

More details:

1. `Dataset$slice()` gets params from `Dataset$get_params()`
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/R-package/R/lgb.Dataset.R#L538
2. `Dataset$get_params()` filters down to only Dataset-specific parameters.
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/R-package/R/lgb.Dataset.R#L583-L592
3. `verbose` is not in the Dataset-specific parameters.
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/R-package/R/aliases.R#L9
4. `Dataset$slice()` eventually calls `Dataset$construct()` which calls `LGBM_DatasetGetSubset()`, which runs `config.Set()`
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/src/c_api.cpp#L1407
5. `config.Set()` calls `Log::ResetLogLevel()` which resets log level **globally** for the current process.
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/src/io/config.cpp#L241-L249
6. If `verbosity` isn't found in `params`, it defaults to INFO-level
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/include/LightGBM/config.h#L543

`Dataset.subset()` in the Python package doesn't suffer from this issue because it references `self.params` (the dictionary of parameters stored as an attribute on the `Dataset` instance) instead of using `Dataset.get_params()`, so `verbose` is not filtered out.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/python-package/lightgbm/basic.py#L1860

At first, I tried to just fix this by adding `"verbosity"` to the list of parameters returned by `Dataset$get_params()`. Unfortunately, that can lead to a new type of problem where the value of `verbosity` stored in a constructed Dataset will take precedence over values passed to `lgb.train()` / `lgb.cv()` if that Dataset is re-used multiple times.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/R-package/R/lgb.Booster.R#L42

Next, I tried just changing the use of `Dataset$get_params()` to use `Dataset$private$params` in `Dataset$slice()`. That uncovered another bug. That still suffered from the problem that once the training Dataset was constructed, verbosity was locked in to the value from the last time it was constructed.

1. When calling `Dataset$construct()`, if it's already been constructed then R calls `LGBM_DatasetUpdateParamChecking()`
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/R-package/R/lgb.Dataset.R#L562
2. That call is try-catched. If it fails, then `Dataset$construct()` does the right thing based on whether or not raw data is still available. However, if that call does NOT fail, `private$params` is not updated!
    * https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/af5b40e1f653e25a5f3470e09de4d1c68dc59663/R-package/R/lgb.Dataset.R#L559-L577

A specific (admittedly, somewhat contrived) example I can think of is:
* Some checked-in file contains a "git branch name which should be used to fetch golden test files"
* Some uncacheable rule will run `git` to get the contents of a file at that sha. It depends on reading that file, to construct the command line to run. Something like:
```python
git_branch_name = read(some_path_in_git)
golden_value = execute("git", "show", f"{git_branch_name}:golden_file")
use_golden_value()
```

As I believe the current PR stands, if this rule is marked as once-per-session, and the contents of `some_path_in_git` changes, we won't re-run this rule.

It's unclear whether we should.
I can argue that we shouldn't re-run (because in intent, "once-per-session" isn't actually an "exactly-once" guarantee, it's an "at-least once" guarantee - the point of the feature is to say "Yeah, this otherwise would be a cache hit, but we're going to pretend it wasn't because we know it reads external state").
I can argue that it should (because something regular which it depends on was invalidated, and that's how invalidation generally works).

Not allowing deps feels like a safe thing to do, because forcing people to decompose their rules to be one of "uncacehable, but no deps" or "if any of its deps change, it will rerun" seems reasonable. (Except ugh then goal_rules still need to be special).
Re-running feels like a safe thing to do, because we're saying these things _shouldn't_ be side-effecting, so it's _safe_ for us to run these things as often as we want.
Allowing deps, but not re-running, feels like it opens up a chance for stale results and unsurprising behaviour.

Semi-nit: it looks like `PurePath` would work for everything in this file and I recommend changing to it. `PurePath` is a superclass of `Path` and has all the same methods as `Path`, except for methods that actually inspect the real filesystem. For example, there is no `PurePath.exists()` or `PurePath.is_file()`.

Why use `PurePath`? It ensures we don't accidentally leak into the filesystem when we didn't intend to and it better expresses the intent of the code, that you're solely doing path manipulation and not actually reading from the filesystem.

Systematically prefixing can be advantageous in particular cases like Numpy methods, but I wouldn't make it a general rule. It feels burdensome.

Yeah, I understand and I agree with you that it's in a lot of cases better to explicitly list out arguments so that things like IDEs can give proper hints on what arguments should be passed in.

In this case, the biggest concern would be the API exposed to the end user, or the programmer using pypika in their project. When using a function, they would see the `__init__` function arguments, therefore it's a good idea to list of those arguments explicitly so that they would see what is expected. 

On the other hand, with the `get_sql` function, there's a trade off of having to maintain a LOT more code if we need to do it for ever SQL function we implement (obviously there're a lot of them). In that case, since most SQL functions have the same syntax, `func_name(arg0, arg1, etc)`, we don't need to implement `get_sql` for this every time. So here we have a balance between generalizing the code to reduce duplication. while also exposing the arguments explicitly to the user of the Function class we're creating. I think this is a pretty good compromise. 

One other thing worth noting, since pypika build a recursive tree of elements which it then serializes to SQL, there are a couple of arguments which it needs to pass through this hierarchy, such as the quote_char, and if anywhere in the tree one of these arguments gets lost in one of the branches of the tree, then it will lead to incorrect SQL for the children nodes. This can take a bit of time to debug, because it might not be immediately obvious in which class the argument was dropped. The less custom implementations of `get_sql` we can write, the better, because each implementation of it introduces a new place where bugs can hide.

> Is the same problem not true of array, for example if the user passes single-precision float?

Assuming I understand you correctly I'd argue that this is a special case due to a "quirk" in NumPy. Each element in a boolean array actually takes up one full byte (same as uint8). Because we made sure that mask is a boolean array we can safely reinterpret that by creating a view showing us `True` as `1` and `False` as `0` which is just how those values are actually stored in memory.

`array` is a different case because `view` behaves differently for numeric dtypes. Creating views between dtypes with a different itemsize only works by changing the number of elements in the array which we clearly don't want here. Furthermore interpreting say an int64 as a float64 will get interesting but in this case not really useful results. E.g. `np.array([3], "i8").view("f8")` will return `array([1.5e-323])`.

> Also, regarding "combining the nested if statements", this would require something like iterate_and_store(..., mask=mask.view(np.uint8) if mask is not None else None), so I just stuck with the nested if.

There seems to be some room for error here. On the test deployment on SM, the gpu-worker-manager is stuck in a loop of no progress made while bundles are waiting staged. 

I think one plausible scenario for this is:
1. Worker manager is started, there are neither staged bundles nor worker jobs
2. Worker manager launches 1 worker job (to reach `min_workers`)
3. `wait_for_progress` is set to `True` since a new worker was launched
4. A run bundle is submitted and assigned to this newly created worker
5. Worker manager starts its new iteration, but isn't aware that its recently created worker picked up a bundle, because that bundle was never in its `staged_uuids` list
6. Another run bundle is submitted to Codalab and starts waiting in the `staged` state.
7. The worker manager refuses to launch another worker job despite the staged bundle because it doesn't think any progress was made on its first worker.
8. The second run bundle has to wait for the first run bundle to finish before it can be run.

The problem here is that a bundle might get submitted and immediately assigned to a new worker before the worker-manager can register it as a staged bundle first and then realize it's no longer staged (ie from the worker manager's perspective, its latest worker is doing nothing, and the staged bundles cannot be assigned to it for some mysterious reason).

Right now I'm trying to wrap my head around whether we can get to such a degenerate state some other way, but this is definitely one way the current system can get stuck.

Okay, here’s a script to find offenders (needs Python 3.8 for recent
changes to the `ast` module):

```python
import ast
import glob
import os


def main():
    for filename in glob.glob("tensorboard/**/*.py", recursive=True):
        with open(filename) as infile:
            source = infile.read()
        sourcelines = source.splitlines()
        module = ast.parse(source, filename=filename)
        strings = [x for x in ast.walk(module) if isinstance(x, ast.Str)]
        for node in strings:
            base = _indent_of(sourcelines[node.lineno - 1])
            lines = node.s.splitlines()
            if len(lines) <= 1:
                continue
            if any(_indent_of(x) < base for x in lines[1:] if x.strip()):
                print("%s:%d" % (filename, node.lineno))


def _indent_of(line):
    n = 0
    for c in line:
        if c != " ":
            break
        n += 1
    return n


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

    t0 = datetime_module.datetime(2015, 7, 4, 12, 0, 0)
    t0_utc = pytz.UTC.localize(datetime_module.datetime(2015, 7, 4, 12, 0, 0))

    assert as_datetime(t0) == t0
    assert as_datetime(t0, tz=pytz.UTC) == t0_utc
    assert not as_datetime('2015-07-04T12:00:00').tzinfo
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04T12:00:00') == t0
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04 12:00:00') == t0
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04 12:00:00', tz=pytz.UTC) == t0_utc
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04 12:00:00Z').tzinfo
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04 12:00:00Z') != t0
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04 12:00:00Z') == t0_utc
    assert as_datetime('2015-07-04 12:00:00-0000') == t0_utc
```

I strongly oppose having the delivery report perform deserialization: just pass the original object to the delivery report callback instead.
It seems like an anti-pattern to me to mix the serialized form/output with the internal program logic.

As for key() and value() changing meaning: yes, that is a problem, and it would be possible to change the semantics of key() and value() in the delivery report callback when using the SerializingProducer(), but then we're also always holding on to the passed object which has GC and lifetime issues which might not always be desired.

So I'd say we keep it simple for now and simply document that key() and value() are the serialized form iin the delivery report callback, and to use bound variables to pass the object as an opt-in if the original object is needed in the dr cb.

Yeah, I think we need to expose the `creation_options` tuple as well.  As implemented, the defaults look like this in `geoprocessing_core.pyx`:

```python
DEFAULT_CREATION_OPTIONS = ('TILED=YES', 'BIGTIFF=YES', 'COMPRESS=LZW',
                             'BLOCKXSIZE=256', 'BLOCKYSIZE=256')
INT8_CREATION_OPTIONS = DEFAULT_CREATION_OPTIONS + (
    'PIXELTYPE=SIGNEDBYTE',)
```

The likely conflict here is that `DEFAULT_CREATION_OPTIONS` are GTiff-specific (here's the [ERS format](https://gdal.org/drivers/raster/ers.html), for example, with many fewer creation options).

`PIXELTYPE=SIGNEDBYTE` is a GDAL implementation detail that happens to be consistent across the data formats that support signed bytes, which are [EEDAI, ERS, GTA, GTiff, HFA, netCDF, PostGISRaster, EHDR, RRaster, Rasterlite2 and Zarr.](https://gdal.org/development/rfc/rfc87_signed_int8.html#details).

So, it makes sense that `PIXELTYPE=SIGNEDBYTE` would be added to the needed creation options in certain cases, but the base set of creation options should be specific to the target datatype, and since the user can change the datatype they should also be able to change the creation options.

You seem to misunderstand how inheritence works in object-oriented programming.

- `serial.rfc2217.Serial.__init__`  calls `super(Serial, self).__init__()` which results in this function, `SerialBase.__init__`.
- `SerialBase.__init__()` calls `self.open()`
  - `self` still refers to a `serial.rfc2217.Serial` object, so `open` (due to method resolution order) refers to `serial.rfc2217.Serial.open()` (the function you want it to call).

If you remove this call to `self.open()`, how would any of the other classes derived from `SerialBase` actually open their serial ports?

Here we are already using reference counting, plus a limit on the total size of the objects.

here are how it works currently:
1. Use reference counting on the objects returned from `ray.get`, when the reference count drops to zero, the object is automatically deleted. This means objects will get deleted immediately after they are used.
2. The store has a limit of the size of total objects, and it maintains a list of objects that haven't been used, these objects can be evicted in FIFO order when total object size reaches the limit. 

As I understand your suggestion is to remove rule 2 above, so that objects remain in store as long as it haven't been `ray.get` from worker right?  A possible issue is that user calls a large number of remote calls but doesn't call `ray.get`, then the store memory can grow unlimited. Should we handle this case?

Inplace and no-op behavior is not the same. inplace means that a computation will be executed, but you can choose where the result is stored. no-op means that depending on the input maybe nothing will be done and the input is returned. Otherwise you get a new storage and thus the old one in not modified inplace.

Example for the latter:
```pycon
>>> t = torch.empty((), dtype=torch.float32)
>>> assert t.to(torch.float32) is t
```

That being said, I think it is a reasonable question to ask, if we want no-op functionals. I think this is what @vfdev-5 touched upon in #7518. I don't have a strong opinion here in general.

The requirement is that `old_labels` be an iterable over the old labels with fast `v in old_labels` checks. In python3 we use `mapping.keys()` to make explicit that we only care about the keys and because in python3, `.keys()` returns a _view_ rather than a new object (which would mean a copy). The dict itself also qualifies, but is less explicit because it has some other stuff (the keys/new labels) hanging around. But since in python2, `.keys()` returns a list, which fails the " fast `v in old_labels` " criteria, we go with the less explicit option.

The idea for this behavior of address callback (if I recall) is to make the user/host side code simpler on what to do on receiving the `callback`:
- Anytime the callback is received, we know it's an internal OT address, so should be safe to add/remove the same address on host (linux) interface.
- Anytime an external address is being added, host would know the origin (if it is already from linux interface or not) and can take the proper action (add it on both OT and linux interface or just on OT).

If we change the code to invoke the callback on all addresses (external and internal), the code handling it on host side would be more complex and host on rx of callback needs to decide whether it would need to update the linux interface or not (there are race timing issues (adding/removing address on linux netif can take time) to consider as well).

in general, stylistically, we generally prefer to have variables keep what their semantics are throughout a function. in this case "notebook" means "notebook name or filename" and past line 66 it doesn't mean that anymore. That means anyone coming back could start using that variable after line 66 and not realize that it has been mutated. So when you need to mutate a variable we generally prefer making a new variable for that new value. This isn't that "Pythonic" and is more functional in style, but it leads to be more predictable code. ```

```python
assert len(results) == 8
assert results[0].id == 1
assert results[1].id == 3
assert results[2].id == 7
assert results[3].id == 5
assert results[4].id == 2
assert results[5].id == 6
assert results[6].id == 4
assert results[7].id == 8

assert [
    (result.name, result.count, result.id) for result in results
] == [
    ('name_1', 5, 1),

    ('name_1', 2, 7),
    ('name_1', 2, 5),
    ('name_2', 10, 2),

It would worry me that an unintended side-effect is for the function to block (waiting for stdin), as I assume this would catch many people by surprise, may be difficult to debug (e.g., a CI build hanging, or tox randomy hanging with no stdout/in redirected anywhere).

I'm not entirely sure what's the user story we have in mind, but for a library that is generating keys in a program, it also feels like this is a feature that many people may not want. This is reminiscent of that time we added colorama, and all the consequences that ensured (i.e., very little benefit, and a lot of maintenance burden).

Although I agree that secure by default is a thing, I also wonder if this is adding meaningful security for most of the usecases. I'd expect the following:

- The API function to be noninteractive (hell, we don't even know if this is going to be attached to a terminal)
- A CLI tool that uses this function to set up a prompt (or use this batteries-included setting). 

I don't think at the time of writing this function we have enough context to know how users will use this regularly, so I err on the side of non-interactivity by default.

Sure, I added comments to the code which should help clarify things.
HLS has two types of playlists, a master playlist and regular playlists. The master playlist usually contains multiple variant streams and metadata which describes them (including bandwidth and resolution). The players can then choose between the variants depending on available resources.
The stream component originally just used a single regular playlist since we only have one stream variant. In 0.115, we made several changes to stream, including not including an audio stream with the video stream when there is no audio. However, this made chromecast devices not play the stream. We realized that we could fix around this problem by making a master playlist with only our single variant regular playlist with metadata which indicated that the stream had only video and no audio.
After this change, the stream component now sends a master playlist instead of a regular playlist. This is slightly less efficient since a master playlist must be loaded and parsed before the regular playlist can be loaded and played. Using a master playlist did help us by giving the ability to check the codec of the stream so we could restrict ExoPlayer use to only videos using the H.265 (HEVC) codec. But this actually resulted in 2 loads of the master playlist before the regular playlist load - once by ha-hls-player to check the codec, then once by the player itself. So we effectively went from one load (the player loading the regular playlist) to three loads (ha-hls-player loading the master playlist, the player loading the master playlist, then the player loading the regular playlist).
We previously reduced these 3 loads to 2 loads by passing the regular playlist directly to the player, helping the player avoid loading the master playlist. However, we did this by a simple string replace. @hunterjm noted that this might be undesirable because makes the frontend rely on the path of the variant stream coded in the backend.
This PR just gets the regular playlist correctly by parsing the regular playlist url from the master playlist.

Q1: Do we need to destroy these normal sessions?

The answer is: Yes.

1. If we don't destroy these normal sessions here, then there is no other place to handle these useless sessions (to free the underlying c objects). Unless we keep tracking sessions on a connection, and release all the sessions when we're shutting down a connection.

2. Consider the situation we're using SASLPlain, there would be no cbs session in this case, just normal sessions.

Q2: How the CBS session authenticates other sessions?

I think this is a misunderstanding, I absolutely in favour of the method `add_passthru`, because after getting to know it better, I clearly see the benefit. In the current implementation, it will not through any warning, because it will only interact with the private attribute `_passthru_prefixes`. The desire to deprecate the public attribute/property `passthru_prefixes` (especially the setter) comes from thinking about PEP 20 (Zen of Python): "*There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.*". The method `add_passthru` is already the preferred setter method, so I would discourage to offer this alternative way and therefore remove it from the public interface. Of cause, the property offers the possibility to register multiple passthroughs at once, but a loop just adds one line.

Still, I could imagine the situation, where someone wants to check which passthroughs are already registered. which I addressed with the comment
> If you think there should be a way to query the registered Passthrough responses, I might implement something as read only property, but I currently do not see the necessity and would follow the good old practice "if in doubt leave it out".
Therefore, I could imagine to expose the registered passthroughs with a property (only the getter), which instead of the prefixes would provide the corresponding `PassthroughResponse` instances, which facilitates to use the response registry methods (remove, replace, upsert) with them.

Note that the config parameter in each query is optional and caller can choose not to use/provide one.

This model gives the user flexibility to set a default server/config  (e.g. the one used for all DNS-SD queries) so service discovery can be done easier, but still be able to send query to other servers (specify config per query) without changing the default config.

> Remove this argument since the caller can already do everything it want with otDnsClientSetDefaultConfig (which I suggested to rename to otDnsClientSetConfig) ?
> I think we should make the API as simple as possible, unless the benefits are clear.

There is a reason and benefit for this model:
- User (next layer code) may not want to use same server for all query types:
   - e.g., address query may use a differernt name server, compared to the server used for DNS-SD queries (another use-case is nat64 which may require its own address queries).
- If we require all queries to use the default config, then the user need to call multiple APIs whenever she wants to send a query with a different config:
  -  First change the default config, start the query, change it back to original 
  - or user needs to always call two APIs: set the default, then call the query (which defeats the purpose of having a default config).

The database-level autocommit is always disabled with SQLAlchemy, regardless of if the student is doing a transaction themselves. That's partly because we explicitly disable autocommit on the engine, and also because of the line that reads `dbapi_connection.isolation_level = None`. This was preexisting code designed to, in the words of the comment above it, `Disable underlying API's own emitting of BEGIN and COMMIT`. As for why SQLite throws an error here but PostgreSQL and MySQL don't, I'm not sure.

Our "autocommit" simply means our library commits at the end of the execution.

Fwiw, I think using a more descriptive error, like `transactions must be initiated with BEGIN or START TRANSACTION`, for all the databases (rather than relying on SQLite's own more difficult error) is probably just a better user experience.

Asking now is fine! :-) Depending on exactly what you’re asking, I think
maybe the answer is “when we call `bk.to_string()` below”? but let me
provide some more context.

Blob keys have string fields defined as `Cow<'a, str>` because we can
get blob keys in two different ways. First, in `ReadBlobSequences`, we
synthesize blob keys from the runs and tags that we already have in
memory. Second, in `ReadBlob`, we parse a blob key from user input.
When we parse a blob key from a base64 string, we have to allocate new
memory for the eid/run/tag: no way around it, since they’re not stored
anywhere else. But when we’re synthesizing a blob key, we’d like to
avoid cloning the eid/run/tag, since we already have references to them.
This is where the [`Cow<'a, str>`] comes in: you can think of it as
“either an owned string or a borrowed string, plus a bit indicating
which is which so that we know whether to free memory when this is
dropped”.

So on this line, we create a blob key that just points into the existing
string structures. Then, when we create the blob reference below, we
encode it all as base64-over-JSON, which allocates a new `String`.

Maybe this is what you meant by “turn into mutable (`to_mut`)”, or maybe
not; does this help? if not, can you clarify?

Does NumPy really define an ordering on complex numbers?

If users want to sort complex numbers (or find their minimum/maximum) by absolute value or some other criteria, they can construct an intermediate. Calling Python's `abs` on the Awkward Array propagates `np.absolute` to every NumpyArray in the structure, so if the `dtype` is properly preserved as complex, `abs` would automatically give the modulus of all the complex numbers in an array. Then, users can argsort (or argmin/argmax) and apply it the result to the original array. We don't have to provide every option when they can be constructed.

I added this logic for errors that are raised in the case that you have nitems that are less than the size of a block, but span multiple blocks. The `if` clause is encountered on the first block you decompress. It's setting `wanted_decompressed` to the number of items from that first block that are part of nitems, otherwise on following iterations the wanted items for decompression that are read are just set to the number of items in a block since the start point of that block is the first item

For example:
```
z = zarr.zeros((100), chunks=(10))
z[8:12]
```
would failing before I added this logic because the chunk size is 10, and so it would only read the first block and then end because the number read was greater then `nitems` even though the actual `nitems` wanted weren't all read yet. So in this case, line 604 would evaluate to True on the first block and set `wanted_decompressed = 2` which would then allow `while wanted_decompressed<nitems` in line 591 to evaluate to `True` on the next iteration of the loop so it can read then next block to get the 2 items for `nitems` from the following block as well.

@stuhood I think you're missing what's going on here. First and foremost the PR fixed the associated issue and no more. As such, the primary driver is to separate PATH from the interpreter search path. In order. The easy step is using PEX_PYTHON_PATH as the interpreter search path leaving PATH for it's traditional use. I make no attempt to go beyond that status quo for setting PATH; i.e. <PATH> is a local expansion just like it has been for interpreter search paths. It's also a monolithic path that needs to have mixed local and remote values, just like the status qou.

Now, the key thing I think you missed: To bootstrap the PEX without pythons on the PATH, we need to say: `/full/path/to/interpreter my.pex ...`. The binary path lookup rules are used to do that and are handed the python interpreter search path to do that with. Hopefully makes sense now?

Ah, maybe this is a misunderstanding but I suggest this only as example code in the doc part of the function, not as actual implementation. If we want to keep librosa completely out of this, the example code in the doc of this function could also be
```
    >>> reference_timestamps = mir_eval.io.load_events('reference.txt')
    >>> estimated_timestamps = mir_eval.io.load_events('estimated.txt')
    >>> pcs = mir_eval.align.pcs(reference_onsets, estimated_timestamps, duration)
```
with the hint that duration needs to be provided by the user and indicates the total song length.

Btw, the reason why I am not including the duration as another timestamp in the `reference_timestamps` and `estimated_timestamps` is because it creates logic branching in most of the other metrics, where you would then want to ignore it, since it's technically not a timestamp that needs to be predicted. for example, for the percentage of correctly detected timestamps, it doesn't make sense to include the end of the song as a timestamp to be predicted. PCS is a bit special in this sense, for computing the segment overlap you need the end position of the last segment, which is the end of the song.

> (FYI when you subclass a frozen dataclass, you have to also declare it as a frozen dataclass, or else it gets unfrozen)

In my testing today, I was playing with a custom `__hash__` function on frozen dataclasses. But, it looks subclasses that are dataclasses _do not inherit generated functions like `__hash__`_. That's because the `@dataclass` decorator looks in the current class without traversing `__mro__` when determining if a method is defined. Then, if not defined in the current class, it adds it, effectively overwriting the parent `__hash__` method.

I was worried about that because `AsyncFieldMixin` and `Target` both have a custom `__hash__` function, but my worry was irrelevant because Field and Target don't use dataclasses.

The default predicate implementations are all logic and no data. The hash of any dataclass that has no fields is equivalent, which means that the cache key doesn't change when switching between `if_dependencies_field` and `always`. So, I have to use a custom hash that includes the `__class__` (or something), but that hash function is not inheritable (since each subclass has to have the `@dataclass(frozen=True)` decorator). So, I'm leaning away from dataclasses for this, similar to what `Target` and `AsyncFieldMixin` do.

The non-weighted centroid is computed in a much different way, as it's much simpler. It computes the centroid without taking into account spacing, and applies all the corrections at the very end.

When computing the weighted centroid we start from the local (to the prop bbox) centroid, which is already corrected for spacing, and we just need to apply a translation to the coordinate system of the original (full size) image. If we apply the correction again as suggested this would yield wrong coordinates, as the local centroid would be corrected twice.

More in details: the centroid weighted local uses the weighted moments

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/2f9b82ce95e95c558ffe68bced6225c9a4560e31/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py#L638-L660

and the weighted moments just use the moments which take spacing into account (see line 261 in particular):

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/2f9b82ce95e95c558ffe68bced6225c9a4560e31/skimage/measure/_moments.py#L154-L269

There are a few problems here:
1) You could use an f-string
2) It's best to use named placeholders, using numbers hurts readability because it increases the cognitive complexity (the reader would need to match numbers with the format args and count their positions each time)
3) It's an antipattern to pre-render log entries. For any use of `logging`, you should pass a string with placeholders and then, separate values. There are a few reasons for this: (1) the logging framework may be deciding not to output this log entry depending on the configuration on a completely different level so rendering it is wasteful; (2) some tools that collect stats on what logging invocations are hit use the log line to group the data and it works best with a template line being the same and not different every time the control flow hits this place.

The message is "Invalid thread access" from line 122 of MemoryManager.

The MemoryManager is keeping track of which interpreters are sharing memory and uses a ThreadLocal to track which threads those interpreters are valid on. When PyPointer or PyObject gets the thread state for any native operation it is now going through the MemoryManager which will return the thread state if there is an interpreter for that MemoryManager on that thread. If there is no interpreter for a memory manager then the "Invalid thread access" is thrown.

The implementation of the MemoryManager and PyPointer do not specifically behave differently for SubInterpreter vs. SharedInterpreter, But during construction the SharedInterpreters wil use a static MemroyManager that is shared and SubInterpreters will construct a new MemoryManager and this difference in construction causes different behavior.

> # TODO (wardlt): For reasons I don't understand, re-using a configuration is a problem. This creates a fresh one

A Config() object in parsl is not a description of the components (such as executors and launchers) that will be used in a run. It *is* the components that will be used.

So, for example, a config might contain a high throughput executor, and that high throughput executor will be configured with script directories etc, started up, used and then shut down. None of the parsl code is built on the basis that after shutting various components down, you can reconfigure them and start them up again - which is what happens when you re-use a config.

The *description* of the configuration, which *can* be re-used, is the code that generates a Config() object - and the way to re-use that is to put it in a function, so that you can generate a fresh config object when you need to start a new DFK.

In that sense, the "configuration file" equivalent is not the Config object itself, but the source code that generates a config object.

I was exploring this logic when trying to implement making the deploy process walk down the tree of dependencies. I had started with a rule that had a `_DeployTargetDependenciesRequest -> _DeployStepResults` signature that performed the recursive step and I was experiencing an infinite loop in Pants.

I added the conditional check when comparing my implementation with the one used in the JVM and thought that maybe `CoarsenedTarget` includes the representative target in its `dependencies` - as that would explain the infinite loop. It initially didn't solve the infinite loop problem and while I was debugging I could notice that my assumption was wrong: `dependencies` only included the actual dependencies.

The solution to the infinite loop was to break that _recursive_ rule `_DeployTargetDependenciesRequest -> _DeployStepResults` into two rules: `_DeployTargetDependenciesRequest -> _DeployTargetRequests` and `_DeployTargetRequests -> _DeployStepResults`. The conditional we are talking about here was a leftover artifact.

I think there is a race condition here.

Because we do this "fire and forget," there may be a (very rare) situation where we cancel this obj ref and start a new one, but this one actually ends up running and overwriting the goal state. In this case we would have an internal state inconsistency: we think the application is updated but it's actually running a previous version.

This is another reason why we should change the behavior such that the `deploy_obj_ref` _returns_ the goal state and it gets applied here: that way we can reason about and avoid any such race conditions from having multiple writers.

I don't think this is a regression from the current behavior, so I'm ok not fixing it in this PR assuming that you plan to follow up with the fix suggested above before the 2.6 branch cut.

Here's how hook invocation works:
- When `build_hook_context` is called, an `UnboundHookContext` is constructed.
- When that hook context is passed to the invocation of a hook, it is foisted into a `BoundHookContext` object, which has access to the hook's attributes.

As a result, it's necessary to carry forward attributes from `UnboundHookContext` to `BoundHookContext`. You can see where this happens here https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/blob/master/python_modules/dagster/dagster/core/definitions/hook_invocation.py#L28.

Additionally, these attributes will need to be added to the default `UnboundHookContext`, created here:
https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/blob/master/python_modules/dagster/dagster/core/definitions/hook_invocation.py#L17.

Because the test happens in Python land, it _does_ need to be included in our production binary / native extension. Only, both Python and Rust production code should be careful to not use this function.

```
(?:(\w+) ((?:\w+=".*?"(?:, )?)+)(?:, )?)
^1                ^2            ^3
```


1. The surrounding non-capturing group `(?:)` is unnecessary.
2. Non-greedy match `.*?` is slower than greedy match `[^"]*` as it causes recursion.
3. The last `(?:, )?` is already captured by the first `(?:, )?`.

Note: This regex is used for capturing `www-authenticate` which may appear multiple times. Per https://github.com/psf/requests/issues/1415, the values are joined by `, `

https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec4.html#sec4.2

> Multiple message-header fields with the same field-name MAY be present in a message if and only if the entire field-value for that header field is defined as a **comma-separated list**.

However, the format of `WWW-Authenticate` contradicts RFC 2616 (it is not a **comma-separated list**).

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/app-resilience-continuous-access-evaluation#the-code

```
Bearer
  authorization_uri="https://login.windows.net/common/oauth2/authorize",
  error="insufficient_claims",
  claims="eyJhY2Nlc3NfdG9rZW4iOnsibmJmIjp7ImVzc2VudGlhbCI6dHJ1ZSwgInZhbHVlIjoiMTYwNDEwNjY1MSJ9fX0="
```

But the second re-install uses updated pip and correctly builds pip.exe for the target.
